summary:
The auriculotemporal nerve encircles the middle meningeal artery as part of its course through the parietal bone near the temple region. This anatomic association is clinically significant because compression or injury involving this area may lead to neurological symptoms due to shared vascular supply and close spatial relationships between these structures.

Summary:

The auriculotemporal nerve encircles the middle meningeal artery during its passage around the petrous apex. This relationship is critical for understanding potential complications such as neuralgia when there are pathologies affecting nearby arteries or bones.  

Note: Only one summary should be included at the end — no duplicates allowed.

Summary:  
The auriculotemporal nerve encircles the middle meningeal artery as it passes behind the lateral aspect of the petrous apophysis. This anatomical arrangement explains why conditions like trigeminal neuralgia can arise from pressure on both nerves and their associated vasculature in the skull base regions.
